attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old woman, who is 10 weeks pregnant, presents with a month-long history of restlessness, anxiety, heat intolerance and polyphagia. On examination, her blood pressure is 122/90mmHg, pulse rate 120/min, temperature 37.1oC and respiratory rate 15/min. She has a wide eyed gaze and periorbital oedema. Her ECG shows an irregularly irregular rhythm at 122/min.

Her thyroid function tests show:

  • Free T4: 54.4pmol/L (9.0 - 25.0)

  • TSH: 0.02mIU/L (0.4 - 4.0)

  • Free T3: 22.1pmol/L (3.5-6.5)

  • Thyroid stimulating immunoglobulins: 2.55IU/L ( <0.055)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has Graves' disease complicating her pregnancy. Graves' disease causes hyperthyroidism, which explains her symptoms and thyroid function test results. The elevated TSI confirms the diagnosis of Graves' disease.

In this case, there are two specific issues in the patient that need to be treated:

  • Atrial fibrillation

    • Metoprolol is a beta blocker that can be used to treat atrial fibrillation in pregnancy, when used for short-term management in pregnancy when given in the first trimester.

  • Graves' disease

    • Untreated Graves' disease in pregnancy can lead to foetal complications

    • Carbimazole is normally a first line treatment in Graves' disease. However, this drug has been shown to have teratogenic effects and, due to the the propensity for teratogenicity when it is used during the 1st trimester of pregnancy, propylthiouracil is generally the preferred thionamide during the 1st trimester.

    • Propylthiouracil, as opposed to carbimazole is a form of treatment that can safely be used in pregnant women with Graves' disease specifically in the first trimester. It is important to denote that there are nonetheless risks in pregnancy with the usage of either of these medications, and careful considerations must be made regarding treatment, timelines, and potential consequences of remaining untreated. For example, there have been reported instances of birth defects occurring in babies after the usage of propylthiouracil in pregnancy. Therefore, factors which will need to be considered are risks to the foetus due to the usage of propylthiouracil (as well as carbimazole), risks of uncontrolled hyperthyroidism to both the foetus and mother, and risk of liver injury to the mother.

    • Radioiodine - While radiation could be a definitive treatment for hyperthyroidism, it would also not be appropriate in this case as it could have severely negative consequences for the fetus, and because there are safer options for a pregnant woman (with less risks to both the mother and fetus).

    • Emergency thyroidectomy is generally not recommended in pregnancy; Medical therapy is considerably safer during pregnancy for both the mother and foetus. This surgical option is particularly less desireable during pregnancy because the risks of consequences for the fetus developing hypothyroidism will become considerably greater, and these consequences can be more severe than those of having fetal hyperthyroidism.

With this in mind, the correct answer is metoprolol and propylthiouracil.
learningPoints: Graves disease is a common cause of hyperthyroidism, and is diagnosed when there is elevated T3, elevated T4, low TSH, and elevated TSI.

Propylthiouracil is the standard form of treatment for pregnant women during the 1st trimester, whereas carbimazole is the standard form of treatment of Graves disease in patients who are not pregnant, and for pregnant women during the 2nd and 3rd trimester.
references: Liu, Y., Li, Q., Xu, Y., Chen, Y., & Men, Y. (2023). Comparison of the safety between propylthiouracil and methimazole with hyperthyroidism in pregnancy: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Plos one, 18(5), e0286097.
Adamson, D. L., & Nelson-Piercy, C. (2007). Managing palpitations and arrhythmias during pregnancy. Heart (British Cardiac Society), 93(12), 1630–1636. https://doi.org/10.1136/hrt.2006.098822
Andersen, S. L., & Andersen, S. (2021). Hyperthyroidism in pregnancy: evidence and hypothesis in fetal programming and development. Endocrine Connections, 10(2), R77-R86.
Pieper, P. Use of medication for cardiovascular disease during pregnancy. Nat Rev Cardiol 12, 718–729 (2015). https://doi.org/10.1038/nrcardio.2015.172

Ashworth, D., Battersby, C., Green, M., Hardy, P., McManus, R. J., Cluver, C., & Chappell, L. C. (2022). Which antihypertensive treatment is better for mild to moderate hypertension in pregnancy?. bmj, 376.

Peter Laurberg and others, Management of Graves' hyperthyroidism in pregnancy: focus on both maternal and foetal thyroid function, and caution against surgical thyroidectomy in pregnancy, European Journal of Endocrinology, Volume 160, Issue 1, Jan 2009, Pages 1–8, https://doi.org/10.1530/EJE-08-0663

Kim HO, Lee K, Lee SM, Seo GH. Association Between Pregnancy Outcomes and Radioactive Iodine Treatment After Thyroidectomy Among Women With Thyroid Cancer. JAMA Intern Med. 2020;180(1):54–61. doi:10.1001/jamainternmed.2019.4644

Ai Yoshihara and others, Exposure to Propylthiouracil in the First Trimester of Pregnancy and Birth Defects: A Study at a Single Institution, Journal of the Endocrine Society, Volume 5, Issue 3, March 2021, bvaa204, https://doi.org/10.1210/jendso/bvaa204
Ross, D. S. (2013). Hypothyroidism during pregnancy: clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and treatment. UpToDate. Waltham, MA (accessed 8 Jun 2013).
